topic: Solar elevation from H(r) — globe declination formula smuggled into dome model
our_argument_summary: WIN-056 claims the dome model derives solar elevation from its firmament height function H(r). Our review shows the author's actual formula is φ_sun(t) = 23.45° × sin(2π(t−81)/365), which is the standard globe solar declination formula. The 23.45° value is Earth's axial tilt — a parameter that has no physical meaning on a flat disc. On the dome's own geometry with a local sun at altitude H, solar elevation = arctan(H_sun/d_observer), a completely different mathematical relationship. The author substitutes the globe formula and claims the match. Verdict: Self-Contradicted.

relevant_files: No monitor.py or pull_data.py exists locally (review project is Node.js)
raw-text/04-coordinates.txt (V9 reference system uses 23.44° obliquity explicitly, line 25)
raw-text/05-model.txt (H(r) function definition, sun altitude 5,733 km)
raw-text/07-ai-context.txt (AI context page confirms sun altitude is 'optical illusion')
monitor/changes/2026-04-05T18-24-13Z.json (WIN-056 added in V51.0 batch, evolution notes V50.10 solar elevation)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitoring infrastructure exists for WIN-056. The dome's own V50.6 monitor.py (referenced in polling data but not extracted locally) runs geomagnetic and seismic domains. Solar elevation is a geometric derivation claim, not a continuously monitored value. The V51.0 poll data shows 39 monitored domains in monitor.py — solar elevation is not among them.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring exists. WIN-056 is a static geometric claim on the dome website with no automated validation.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome's AI context page (07-ai-context.txt, rule 11) states: 'The 5,733 km Sun altitude is an OPTICAL illusion from crepuscular ray convergence. Do NOT claim it physically crashes into the firmament (roof is ~4,300 km).' This reframes the sun/firmament collision as an optical effect via aetheric refraction, allowing the model to maintain contradictory altitude values. The same aetheric refraction escape could be invoked to explain why the globe's declination formula 'works' — refraction bending light such that the apparent elevation matches 23.45° sin function.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not make specific claims about dome monitoring code for this WIN, which is appropriate.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py file exists in the review repository. The dome's own monitor.py (39 domains as of V51.0 polling) handles geomagnetic, seismic, and Schumann data — solar elevation is not a monitored domain. WIN-056 is a static geometric derivation claim added in V51.0 with no automated validation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Solar declination δ = 23.45° × sin(2π(t−81)/365) is a standard approximation of solar declination used in solar energy engineering and astronomy. The exact value ε = 23.4393° (J2000.0) is Earth's axial obliquity, measured since Eratosthenes (~240 BC) and refined to arcsecond precision by modern astrometry. The dome model adopts this formula verbatim and relabels its origin from 'axial tilt of a sphere' to 'property of H(r) density function.'
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Solar declination as a function of day-of-year has been known since antiquity. The specific sinusoidal approximation with 23.45° amplitude is textbook solar engineering (e.g., Duffie & Beckman, 'Solar Engineering of Thermal Processes', 1st ed. 1980). The dome model claimed this as WIN-056 in V51.0 (April 2026), ~46 years after the formula's standard textbook form and millennia after the underlying observation.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The formula φ_sun(t) = 23.45° × sin(2π(t−81)/365) contains no dome parameters. It does not use disc_radius (20,015 km), H(r) = 8537×exp(−r/8619), sun_altitude (5,733 km), or any other dome-specific value. The 23.45° is Earth's axial tilt — physically meaningful only for a tilted sphere orbiting the Sun. On a flat disc with a local sun, solar declination would depend on the sun's radial position on the disc, the firmament height at that radius, and observer position — none of which appear in the formula. The V9 coordinate system (04-coordinates.txt line 25) explicitly uses 23.44° in lat_S = 90 − noon_altitude_june + 23.44, confirming the globe value is used as a direct input, not derived.

holes_found.description: Broken cross-reference to Section 4.5.7: WIN-056's detail_evidence says 'See Section 4.5.7 for the derivation.' In the HTML (docs/index.html), Section 4.5.7 is titled 'Eclipse Duration: Dome vs. Globe' — it discusses eclipse geometry, NOT solar elevation. The solar elevation analysis DOES exist in the DOCX (build-doc-v4.js line 744: '4.5.7 Solar Elevation: Using the Globe's Formula') but was never added to the HTML. The HTML sections 4.5.1–4.5.9 have different content than the DOCX sections. A reader clicking the Section 4.5.7 link in WIN-056 will land on an unrelated eclipse discussion. This is a credibility issue — it looks like we didn't proofread our own cross-references.
holes_found.recommendation: Either: (a) Add a solar elevation section to the HTML's Part 4.5 (probably as 4.5.10 or by renumbering), containing the globe formula analysis from build-doc-v4.js lines 744-746. Then update the cross-reference in WIN-056. Or (b) Remove the cross-reference from WIN-056's detail_evidence and include the derivation argument directly in the WIN-056 detail text. Option (a) is better — the argument is strong enough to warrant its own self-consistency section.

holes_found.description: Unverifiable quote attribution: Our detail_evidence states 'The model page states this "reproduces the globe formula exactly."' This quote cannot be verified from any of our extracted V50.6 raw text (raw-text/01-home.txt through 07-ai-context.txt). WIN-056 was added in V51.0, and the specific page text containing this claim was not extracted into our raw-text archive. If the dome page doesn't actually use these exact words, we are putting words in the author's mouth — a strawman that a defender would immediately flag.
holes_found.recommendation: Either: (a) Verify this quote against the current V51.0 page text and add it to the raw-text archive, or (b) Rephrase to avoid direct quotation: 'The author acknowledges this formula matches the globe model's predictions — because it IS the globe model's formula.' This makes the same point without relying on an unverified quote.

holes_found.description: WIN-056 listed as 'relying on aetheric refraction' in Section 4.9 (escape hatch analysis): The HTML (line 1474) states 'At least six WINs rely on aetheric refraction to avoid falsification: WIN-016 (annual aberration), WIN-017 (stellar parallax), WIN-026 (crepuscular rays), WIN-033 (Sigma Octantis dimness), WIN-056 (solar elevation), WIN-065 (Polaris excess).' But WIN-056's primary argument is about formula substitution (using 23.45° globe declination), not aetheric refraction. Including WIN-056 in the refraction list conflates two distinct issues. A defender could argue we're confused about our own critique — is WIN-056 about refraction or formula substitution?
holes_found.recommendation: Review whether WIN-056 genuinely depends on aetheric refraction. The connection would be: the dome needs refraction to explain why apparent solar elevation matches the globe formula despite different geometry. This is a valid secondary argument but shouldn't be the primary categorization. If kept in the list, add a brief note: 'WIN-056 (solar elevation — dome's geometry predicts different elevation angles; matching the globe formula requires invoking refractive corrections).'

holes_found.description: Claim text says 'density function H(r)' — H(r) is a HEIGHT function, not a density function. From the dome's own site (05-model.txt, 04-coordinates.txt), H(r) = 8537 × exp(−r/8619) km is explicitly the firmament HEIGHT as a function of radial distance. Calling it a 'density function' mischaracterizes what H(r) represents. If this wording comes from the dome's V51.0 text, that's fine (it's their error to report). But if we introduced the wrong term, it undermines precision.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify against V51.0 source text. If the dome calls it 'density function,' note that misidentification. If we introduced the term, correct to 'height function H(r)' or 'firmament height function H(r).'

holes_found.description: Framing inconsistency between HTML and DOCX: The DOCX (build-doc-v4.js line 495) frames WIN-056 as: 'H(r) was fitted to match solar observations, then solar elevation is re-derived from H(r). This is circular.' The HTML detail focuses on globe formula substitution (23.45° axial tilt). These are related but distinct arguments: circularity (fitting then re-deriving) vs. substitution (using globe formula verbatim). Both are valid, but presenting different primary arguments in different outputs could confuse readers who consult both.
holes_found.recommendation: Align the framing. The globe formula substitution argument is stronger and more specific — it identifies the exact smuggled parameter (23.45°). The DOCX summary should match: 'Uses the globe's 23.45° declination formula instead of deriving elevation from dome geometry.' The circularity argument (H(r) fitted → re-derived) can be a secondary point.

holes_found.description: V9 coordinate evidence not cited: The dome's own V9 coordinate system (04-coordinates.txt line 25) uses 'lat_S = 90 − noon_altitude_june + 23.44' — explicitly incorporating the globe's obliquity value as a direct input. This is smoking-gun evidence that the dome model treats 23.44/23.45° as a known constant rather than deriving it from dome geometry. Our WIN-056 detail doesn't mention this.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence: 'The dome's own V9 coordinate system uses lat_S = 90 − noon_altitude + 23.44°, treating Earth's obliquity as a direct input rather than a derived quantity. This confirms the globe's axial tilt value is imported, not derived from dome parameters.'

stronger_arguments.description: Quantitative dome geometry contradiction: On the dome's flat disc with a local sun at altitude H_sun, the solar elevation at any point is θ = arctan(H_sun / d), where d is the horizontal distance from observer to the sun's ground-track point. For the dome's sun at 5,733 km: at Chapel Hill (r ≈ 5,960 km from pole), if the sun is directly overhead at the equator (r ≈ 10,000 km), d ≈ 4,040 km, giving θ = arctan(5733/4040) = 54.8°. The globe's equinox noon elevation at 36°N latitude is 54.0°. Superficially close — but the dome formula has NO seasonal variation mechanism from geometry alone. The globe's 23.45° seasonal swing comes from axial tilt relative to an orbital plane — a concept that doesn't exist on a flat disc. The dome must either (a) move the sun radially across the disc by ~3,000 km between solstices (which would produce a TANGENT function, not a SINE function, for seasonal elevation change) or (b) import the globe's sine formula. The author chose (b).
sources: Dome parameters from 05-model.txt: sun altitude 5,733 km, disc radius 20,015 km
04-coordinates.txt: Chapel Hill r ≈ 5,960 km, Polaris elevation 36.18°

stronger_arguments.description: Mathematical proof that dome geometry cannot produce 23.45° sinusoidal declination: On a flat disc, if the sun moves in a circle at radius r_sun(t) varying seasonally, the elevation angle from an observer at fixed position (r_obs, θ_obs) is: α = arctan(H_sun / sqrt(r_obs² + r_sun² − 2·r_obs·r_sun·cos(Δθ))). This is an ARCTANGENT of a SQUARE ROOT expression — mathematically incompatible with the observed sinusoidal declination δ(t) = 23.45° × sin(ωt). The dome geometry produces elevation angles that depend on observer position in a fundamentally non-linear way that cannot reduce to a simple sine function of time for all observer locations simultaneously. This is not a matter of parameter tuning — the functional form is wrong.
sources: Elementary trigonometry of elevation angle on a plane

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model's H(r) = 8537 × exp(−r/8619) km is a genuinely clever mathematical construction. It successfully reconciles three different height measurements (Schumann ~9,500 km near pole, Polaris ~4,750 km at mid-latitudes, model average ~9,086 km) into a single exponential curve. This is legitimate curve-fitting — the author identified an apparent discrepancy in their own framework and resolved it with a unified function. The attempt to derive solar elevation from this geometric framework is also the RIGHT theoretical approach — if the dome model is to be self-consistent, it SHOULD predict observable quantities from its own parameters rather than importing them.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The problem is execution, not ambition. Having constructed H(r), the author needed to derive solar elevation from dome geometry: θ = f(H_sun, r_obs, r_sun(t), H(r)). Instead, they substituted the globe's formula φ_sun(t) = 23.45° × sin(2π(t−81)/365), which contains a parameter (23.45° axial tilt) that has no physical origin in dome geometry. A flat disc does not have an 'axial tilt' — it has a sun at some altitude traveling some path. The functional form of solar elevation on a flat disc is fundamentally different from the globe's sinusoidal declination. The H(r) framework, however clever, does not produce 23.45° from first principles. The author's own coordinates page (V9) treats 23.44° as a direct numerical input, confirming it is imported rather than derived.

deeper_analysis:
The Self-Contradicted verdict is correct and well-chosen. The core argument — that the dome model uses the globe's 23.45° declination formula while claiming a dome-native derivation — is sound and clearly stated. This is one of the most transparent cases of 'formula substitution' in the entire review, and the author's own V9 coordinate system (explicitly using 23.44°) provides direct evidence.

However, I found a major cross-reference error that needs immediate fixing. WIN-056's detail_evidence directs readers to 'Section 4.5.7 for the derivation.' In the published HTML, Section 4.5.7 is 'Eclipse Duration: Dome vs. Globe' — completely unrelated to solar elevation. The solar elevation analysis exists in the DOCX (build-doc-v4.js Section 4.5.7) but was never added to the HTML's Part 4.5 section. This is a discrepancy between the two output formats that needs to be resolved. Either the HTML needs a solar elevation self-consistency section, or the cross-reference needs updating.

The unverifiable quote ('reproduces the globe formula exactly') is also concerning. Our review attributes these words to 'the model page' but they don't appear in any of our V50.6 raw text extractions, and the V51.0 page text for this specific section hasn't been archived. If the dome site doesn't actually say this, we're strawmanning.

A smart dome defender's best response: 'The 23.45° value is not imported from the globe model. It is the maximum angular displacement of the sun's spiral path on the disc, measured from the observer's frame. Our H(r) framework produces this angular displacement through aetheric refraction — light from the sun at different seasonal positions is refracted by the varying aetheric density described by H(r), producing an apparent declination that follows a sinusoidal pattern with 23.45° amplitude. The mathematical equivalence to the globe formula is a prediction of our model, not a borrowed parameter.'

Our counter: (1) No such derivation exists on the dome site — the formula is stated, not derived. (2) Even if aetheric refraction could produce apparent sinusoidal declination, the specific amplitude of 23.45° would need to emerge from dome parameters (H₀=8537, λ=8619, H_sun=5733), not be inserted as a constant. (3) The V9 coordinate system uses 23.44° as a DIRECT NUMERICAL INPUT in the lat_S formula, confirming it was never derived. (4) The sinusoidal functional form arctan-of-distance on a flat plane is mathematically incompatible with simple sine-of-time — the dome's geometry predicts a different function shape, not just different parameter values.

Overall verdict assessment: Self-Contradicted is correct because the dome's own stated geometry (local sun, flat disc, H(r) firmament) implies a specific mathematical relationship for solar elevation (arctan function of distances) that is fundamentally different from the adopted globe formula (sine of time with 23.45° amplitude). Using both simultaneously is a self-contradiction. The verdict is stronger than 'Misleading' because the dome's geometry actively predicts the WRONG functional form.

citations_verified: NASA planetary fact sheet link (https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/earthfact.html) — this is the correct URL for NASA's Earth fact sheet, which lists obliquity of 23.44° (consistent with 23.45° rounded). URL structure is valid for NSSDC Goddard.
citations_unchecked: The cross-reference to 'Section 4.5.7' — this is an internal link (#part4b with onclick to show selftest tab). As noted in holes_found, this link targets the wrong section in the HTML. The DOCX version does contain the correct solar elevation analysis at section number 4.5.7.

The detail_evidence quote 'The model page states this "reproduces the globe formula exactly"' — attributed to the dome's model page but cannot be verified against any text in our raw-text/ archive. Needs verification against current V51.0 site content.
